claim: Compensating engineers by output (story points, completed features) rather than hourly rate creates alignment: high-performing engineers with AI leverage can earn over $1M annually while companies benefit from 10x productivity
actionable: If hiring AI engineers or building teams for rapid development, shift from hour-based to output-based compensation (story points, completed features) to incentivize leverage and speed over billable hours.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: Downsizing engineering teams by 90% while re-architecting workflows to be AI-first can actually increase production-ready software output 10x, inverting the traditional constraint from headcount to process
actionable: If facing headcount constraints, explore AI-native architecture (better tooling, spec-driven development) before scaling headcount; the leverage may come from workflow redesign.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: TypeScript (both frontend and backend) with strong type constraints is the preferred stack for AI agent autonomy because types and error messages allow agents to self-iterate and fix mistakes without human intervention
actionable: When building systems for AI agents to modify, standardize on TypeScript (frontend and backend) with shared type definitions to maximize agent ability to self-correct via compiler errors.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: Best AI engineering outcomes come from hiring people who are either long-term selfish (understand client retention > short-term gaming metrics) OR intrinsically motivated to write great code; both reduce perverse incentive abuse
actionable: When evaluating output-based compensation, hire for character (long-term thinking or intrinsic motivation) not just skill; gaming story points is technically easy but kills clients and careers.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: Running multiple parallel coding agents and verifying them simultaneously drains human attention rapidly; the bottleneck is no longer model speed but human oversight and decision-making capacity
actionable: When scaling agentic workflows, build monitoring and verification gates to reduce human involvement per agent; unlimited agents without attention gates leads to burnout.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: Controlling entropy in autonomous coding loops requires near-perfect error handling: even 1% error rates compound and derail agents, making error correction and verification gates critical
actionable: Design agentic coding systems with multiple verification layers (lint, tests, browser verification, critic agents) and error bounds that catch entropy buildup before it cascades.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: The hardest problem in building autonomous AI software engineers is not model intelligence but context engineering: knowing what context to feed the model and ensuring it attends to the right parts
actionable: Invest in context engineering infrastructure (good file organization, clear abstractions, focused prompts) over just scaling model size; attention mechanisms are the real constraint.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex

claim: Unreasonably difficult take-home interviews for AI engineers (deliberately harder than typical SDE interviews) correlate with finding high-performer candidates; 50% don't complete them, but finalists are stronger
actionable: When hiring AI engineers, use deliberately challenging take-homes that stretch beyond standard SDE interviews; the high filter rate finds people comfortable with novel, complex problems.
source_title: 10x AI Engineers with $1m Salaries — Alex Lieberman & Arman Hezarkhani, Tenex
